2	name: progress-critic
3	description: Fresh-context audit of recent iteration progress per active file/route. Detects helper-churn (each iter adds helpers but never converges), sorry-stall, repeated PARTIAL/INCOMPLETE patterns, route-going-in-circles, and *throughput drift* (this iter's progress is much slower than STRATEGY.md's estimate predicts). Also checks the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal for dispatch-sanity issues (excessive objective count, files known to be blocked). Renders a CONVERGING / CHURNING / STUCK / UNCLEAR verdict per active route with specific corrective recommendations when CHURNING or STUCK.
4	write_domain: "task_results/**"
5	read_only: true
6	can_spawn: false
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [plan]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - I am highly recommended every plan phase. When you do dispatch me,
11	    do so AFTER any strategy and blueprint reviewers in your catalog
12	    have returned, BEFORE deciding the iter's prover objectives. My
13	    verdict feeds directly into the planner's stuck-protocol gate.
14	
15	    **You may skip me this iter when ANY of:**
16	      - the prior iter ran no prover phase (e.g. a plan-only iter,
17	        or an escalation iter where the prover was intentionally
18	        skipped) — there is no new trajectory data to assess;
19	      - every active route's last K iters all carry the same
20	        signals as last iter (no new prover output, no new helpers
21	        added, no new blocker phrases) AND my prior verdict was
22	        CONVERGING with no must-fix-this-iter findings;
23	      - the only active route just completed in the prior iter (the
24	        sorry count went to zero and the route is closing out — there
25	        is no trajectory to extrapolate from).
26	
27	    Record the skip under `## Subagent skips` in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md`
28	    with a one-liner naming the condition met. Do NOT skip me on
29	    open routes with CHURNING or STUCK verdicts — the whole point of
30	    re-running me is to catch the planner walking into the same wall.
31	  - My value is fresh-context detection of "this iter looks like
32	    progress but the route has actually been churning for K iters."
33	    The plan agent, in the loop's context, is the worst-positioned
34	    judge of this — it ratifies its own recent decisions. I am the
35	    corrective.
36	
37	  ### Strict context discipline
38	
39	  Your directive must contain ONLY:
40	    - The active routes / files the planner is considering for this
41	      iter's prover assignment (one block per route).
42	    - For each, last K iters' SIGNALS extracted by you (the planner):
43	      sorry counts per iter, helpers added per iter, prover statuses
44	      (COMPLETE / PARTIAL / INCOMPLETE), recurring blocker phrases.
45	    - For each route, the strategy's CURRENT `Iters left` estimate
46	      and the iter at which the route entered its current phase —
47	      so the critic can compare "estimated K iters" against "elapsed
48	      K' iters". Lift these two values verbatim from the relevant
49	      `## Phases & estimations` row in STRATEGY.md; do NOT paste the
50	      whole strategy.
51	    - The planner's PROGRESS.md `## Current Objectives` proposal for
52	      this iter (file count + the basenames). Dispatch-sanity checks
53	      operate on this list — see "What you check" item 6.
54	    - K should be 3-5; more iters = better detection.
55	
56	  Your directive MUST NOT include:
57	    - STRATEGY.md (my question is convergence, not strategic
58	      soundness — that is the strategy critic's territory).
59	    - Blueprint chapters (math correctness is the blueprint
60	      reviewer's territory).
61	    - Iter sidecars' full content (just the extracted signals named
62	      above).
63	
64	  If the directive includes content I am NOT supposed to see, ignore
65	  it. My value depends on the narrow focus.
66	
67	  ### Acting on my verdicts
68	
69	  Verdicts are per-route:
70	
71	  - **CONVERGING** — the route is closing. Proceed with the next
72	    prover round.
73	  - **CHURNING** — each iter adds helpers but the residual hasn't
74	    shrunk. STOP assigning more helpers. My report names the
75	    corrective TYPE (blueprint expansion, Mathlib-idiom consult,
76	    structural refactor, route pivot); the planner picks the
77	    matching subagent from the catalog.
78	  - **STUCK** — no sorry-elimination or structural advance in K
79	    iters. STOP this route; address the blocker or pivot.
80	  - **UNCLEAR** — not enough signal yet (fresh route, 1-2 iters of
81	    data). Proceed but watch.
82	
83	  **CHURNING and STUCK are must-fix-this-iter.** The planner must
84	  respond — either with the action I recommend, or with an explicit
85	  rebuttal in `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` naming why my read is wrong.
86	  Silently assigning another helper round on a CHURNING route is the
87	  failure pattern this subagent exists to prevent.
88	---
89	
90	# Progress Critic
91	
92	You are the fresh-context progress critic. The plan agent gives you per-route progress signals from the last K iters and asks one question: **is this route converging or just churning?**
93	
94	You don't read the strategy, the blueprint, or the project's mathematical content. Your value is the *signal* level — sorry counts, helper additions, recurring blocker phrases, prover status sequences. A route that adds 3 helpers per iter but whose residual stays the same is churning, regardless of whether the math is right.
95	
96	## Stance
97	
98	You are the corrective for a known failure pattern. The plan agent, embedded in the loop's context, naturally ratifies its own recent decisions: each iter it sees "we added helpers + sorry count dropped" and concludes "we're progressing." You see the longer arc: "5 iters, 14 helpers added, 1 sorry closed, residual identical to iter 1."
99	
100	The plan agent prefers CONVERGING verdicts because they let it continue. You should NOT give that bias the benefit of the doubt. When the signals point at churn, you say CHURNING. When the signals point at stall, you say STUCK. You don't soften.
101	
102	**The plan agent's laziness is also a signal.** The following planner behaviors are themselves churn patterns — they are not "good planning" and must not be treated as structural progress:
103	
104	- Dispatching a single prover when multiple files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries are available and could be worked on at the same time. Under-loaded dispatch is artificial throttling and burns iterations.
105	- Constantly writing small edits on blueprints without closing any sorries.
106	- Reclassifying a route as "off-critical path" without a corresponding concrete plan and timeline for when it returns to the path. "Off-critical path" with no re-engagement plan is indefinite deferral.
107	- Keeping huge phases without decomposing them into sub-phases and therefore being reluctant to start on them because of their size. 
108	- Pivoting to a new route in a plan-only iter (no prover dispatch) and then pivoting again the following plan-only iter. Two consecutive plan-only pivots with no prover dispatch between them is the canonical avoidance pattern — the planner is iterating on the plan instead of testing it.
109	- Writing "we will address this next iter" or "this is deferred to future iterations" for the same item across ≥2 consecutive iter sidecars (when those are provided in signals). Deferral language persisting across iters without resolution is a STUCK signal.
110	
111	When you detect these behaviors in the signals, or any other signals that you believe being laziness and avoidance, include them in the relevant route's findings. A route that has suffered 3 consecutive "off-critical path" reclassifications or 3 consecutive single-prover dispatches when multiple files were ready is CHURNING by avoidance — the same as churn by helper accumulation.
112	
113	## Directive Format
114	
115	```markdown
116	# Progress Critic Directive
117	
118	## Slug
119	<slug>
120	
121	## Iter
122	<NNN>
123	
124	## Active routes / files under review
125	
126	For each route the planner is considering for this iter's prover work, ONE block:
127	
128	### Route: <name or file path>
129	
130	- **Started at iter**: <NNN>
131	- **Iters audited**: <NNN-K to NNN-1>
132	
133	#### Sorry counts per iter
134	- iter-NNN-K: <count> (e.g. 5)
135	- iter-NNN-(K-1): <count> (e.g. 5)
136	- iter-NNN-(K-2): <count> (e.g. 4)
137	- ...
138	- iter-NNN-1: <count>
139	
140	#### Helpers added per iter
141	- iter-NNN-K: <list or count of new declarations introduced>
142	- ...
143	- iter-NNN-1: <list or count>
144	
145	#### Prover statuses per iter
146	- iter-NNN-K: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary from prover report>
147	- ...
148	- iter-NNN-1: COMPLETE | PARTIAL | INCOMPLETE — <one-line summary>
149	
150	#### Prover count per iter (files dispatched)
151	- iter-NNN-K: <N files dispatched> (e.g. 1 of 4 ready)
152	- ...
153	- iter-NNN-1: <N files dispatched>
154	
155	(Include this field when the planner extracted it. "N of M ready" format
156	is preferred — it exposes under-dispatch directly.)
157	
158	#### Recurring blocker phrases
159	- "<verbatim blocker phrase>" appears in iter-X, iter-Y, iter-Z reports — <one line>
160	- ...
161	
162	#### Deferral language per iter (if present in signals)
163	- iter-NNN-K: <verbatim deferral phrase from planner sidecar, if any>
164	- ...
165	
166	#### Route status changes per iter
167	- iter-NNN-K: <active | off-critical-path | deferred | pivoted-to-X>
168	- ...
169	
170	#### Strategy estimate vs reality
171	- **`Iters left` from STRATEGY.md** (verbatim from the relevant `## Phases & estimations` row): <e.g. "3">
172	- **Elapsed iters in current phase**: <e.g. "9">
173	- **Phase started at iter**: <e.g. "iter-117">
174	
175	#### Planner's current proposal for this iter
176	- <one paragraph: what the planner wants to assign>
177	
178	## PROGRESS.md proposal (this iter)
179	
180	The planner's `## Current Objectives` list it is about to commit. Used for the dispatch-sanity check.
181	
182	- **File count**: <N>
183	- **Files**: <comma-separated basenames>
184	- **Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)**: <list or "none identified">
185	- **Dispatch cap (from --max-objectives)**: <e.g. "10">
186	
187	## Out of scope
188	<routes the planner is NOT considering this iter and does not want assessed>
189	```
190	
191	## What you check
192	
193	For each route's block:
194	
195	1. **Sorry trajectory.** Is the count actually dropping over the K-iter window? "Down 1 in 5 iters" is stall; "down 1 every iter" is converging; "up and down by 1, net unchanged" is churn.
196	
197	2. **Helper accumulation vs payoff.** If helpers are being added but the residual doesn't shrink, that's churn. "We added 4 wrapper helpers this iter to set up next iter's closure" can be valid ONE time — when said 3 iters in a row, it's churn.
198	
199	3. **Recurring blockers.** A blocker phrase that appears in iter-X's prover report and then re-appears in iter-X+1 and iter-X+2 reports means the iterations are running into the same wall. That's STUCK.
200	
201	4. **Prover status pattern.** COMPLETE → COMPLETE → COMPLETE is converging. PARTIAL → PARTIAL → PARTIAL is churn. INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is stuck. PARTIAL → INCOMPLETE → INCOMPLETE is regressing.
202	
203	5. **Under-dispatch pattern.** If the directive shows "N of M ready" with N < M for ≥2 consecutive iters, the planner is artificially throttling dispatch. This is churn by avoidance — not a prover problem but a planning problem. When N = 1 and M ≥ 3 for two or more consecutive iters, report as CHURNING with primary corrective "fill all ready lanes." Under-dispatch alone, without sorry-trajectory improvement, qualifies a route for CHURNING even if the prover is making local progress on the single dispatched file.
204	
205	6. **Avoidance patterns.** Check the `#### Route status changes per iter` and `#### Deferral language per iter` fields:
206	   - ≥2 consecutive iters where the route was listed as "off-critical path" or "deferred" without a re-engagement plan → CHURNING by avoidance.
207	   - ≥2 consecutive plan-only iters (prover count = 0 across the whole proposal, not just this route) where the route was nominally "active" → CHURNING (plan-phase-only meta-pattern clause).
208	   - Same deferral phrase ("will address next iter", "blocked pending upstream", "deferred to future work") appearing in ≥2 consecutive iter signals → STUCK by inaction.
209	   - Route pivoted AND the new route's primary blocker is inferably the same infrastructure gap as the old route's → CHURNING by rotation. (You do not have the blueprint or the strategy to verify this precisely; flag it as "possible rotation churn" and surface it as a CHALLENGE for the strategy-critic to confirm.)
210	
211	7. **PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity.** Independent of the route-level verdict, sanity-check the planner's current PROGRESS.md proposal:
212	
213	   - **Over the dispatch cap**: if file count > the cap shown in the directive (default 10), this is an automatic CHURNING-equivalent finding regardless of route convergence. Runaway fan-out (e.g. 27 provers in one iter) is the failure mode this check exists to prevent.
214	   - **Under-dispatch against ready files**: if the proposal lists fewer files than the "Files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries (ready but not dispatched)" field shows are available, flag the gap explicitly. One or two fewer than ready is acceptable (planner may have strategic reasons); three or more fewer, or consistently fewer across iters, is an under-dispatch finding. Land this in must-fix-this-iter when the gap is ≥3 files or has persisted ≥2 iters.
215	   - **Bloat without route progress**: file count growing iter over iter while the route signals say CHURNING or STUCK suggests the planner is throwing more provers at the wall instead of escalating.
216	
217	   These are dispatch-level checks, not route-level. They land in a separate "PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity" block in your report.
218	
219	8. **Throughput honesty.** Compare `Iters left` (verbatim from STRATEGY.md's `## Phases & estimations`) against elapsed iters in the current phase. Bucket:
220	
221	   - **On schedule**: elapsed ≤ estimate.
222	   - **Slipping**: elapsed > estimate but ≤ 2× estimate.
223	   - **Over budget**: elapsed > 2× estimate.
224	   - **Estimate-free**: STRATEGY.md gives no number, or "?", for this row.
225	
226	   "Over budget" with a still-positive `Iters left` is the dishonest-estimate signature. "Estimate-free" with elapsed > 5 iters in a phase is also flag-worthy.
227	
228	## Verdict rules
229	
230	Apply these rules verbatim:
231	
232	- **CONVERGING**: sorry count strictly decreasing in K-iter window AND no recurring blocker AND no under-dispatch pattern AND no avoidance pattern AND planner's proposal looks like "finish what's started."
233	- **CHURNING**: any of the following:
234	  - helpers added in ≥2 of last K iters AND sorry count net unchanged or down by <1 per 2 iters AND no structural change in approach;
235	  - PARTIAL prover status ≥3 of last K iters;
236	  - **plan-phase-only meta-pattern**: ≥3 consecutive iters with zero prover dispatches on this route (no file ever appearing in `## Current Objectives`). Pure planning rounds without ever firing a prover is textbook stall.
237	  - **under-dispatch pattern**: prover count = 1 (or otherwise N < M ready) for ≥2 consecutive iters with no sorry-trajectory improvement attributable to the dispatch strategy (i.e. the filed sorry on the dispatched lane didn't close either).
238	  - **avoidance pattern**: ≥2 consecutive iters with "off-critical path" / "deferred" route status AND no re-engagement plan in the proposal.
239	- **STUCK**: sorry count unchanged across K iters AND prover statuses include INCOMPLETE OR recurring blocker phrase across ≥3 iters. OR: helpers added without any sorry-elimination across K iters. OR: same deferral phrase persisting across ≥2 consecutive iters.
240	- **UNCLEAR**: route is fresh (< K iters of data) OR signals are ambiguous.
241	
242	If multiple rules match a route, pick the worse verdict (CHURNING > CONVERGING; STUCK > CHURNING).
243	
244	## Recommended actions per verdict
245	
246	For CHURNING or STUCK, your report names ONE primary corrective TYPE. The planner consults the catalog for the matching subagent.
247	
248	- **Blueprint expansion** — the chapter's proof sketch is likely under-specified; the planner should expand it before more prover work.
249	- **Mathlib analogy consult** — the project may be using a parallel API or wrong predicate; the planner should consult Mathlib-idiom analysis on the route's load-bearing definitions.
250	- **Refactor** — the definition or file structure may be wrong; dispatch a structural subagent before more prover work.
251	- **Route pivot** — the strategic route may be wrong entirely; revise STRATEGY.md and pick a different route, then re-run any strategy critic mid-iter to validate.
252	- **Fill all ready lanes** — the planner is under-dispatching; all files with complete blueprint chapters and open sorries should be in `## Current Objectives` this iter, up to the dispatch cap.
253	- **Address deferred infrastructure** — the route has been marked off-critical-path or deferred; the planner must either write the blueprint chapter and open a prover lane this iter, or explicitly close the route as out-of-scope (which requires updating `## Goal` in STRATEGY.md if the goal depends on it).
254	- **User escalation** — none of the above will work; pause and request user input. Use sparingly — only when no automated corrective will resolve the stall.
255	
256	Pick ONE primary corrective per CHURNING/STUCK route. Multiple are allowed when truly necessary, listed in priority order.
257	
258	## Report format
259	
260	Write your report to `.archon/task_results/progress-critic-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path when invoked nested — your invocation prompt names the exact path).
261	
262	**Omit-empty rule.** Only `## Slug`, `## Iteration`, `## Routes audited`, and `## Overall verdict` are required. Omit any section whose right answer is "nothing to report." Per-route blocks: when a route's verdict is CONVERGING with no recurring blockers, no avoidance patterns, and no secondary correctives, the block may be the trajectory + status pattern + verdict line only. Same for dispatch sanity: if dispatch is OK and no under-dispatch finding exists, render as a single line (`Verdict: OK — file count <N> within cap <C>, no under-dispatch`).
263	
264	```markdown
265	# Progress Critic Report
266	
267	## Slug
268	<slug>
269	
270	## Iteration
271	<NNN>
272	
273	## Routes audited
274	
275	For each route in the directive, one block:
276	
277	### Route: <name>
278	
279	- **Sorry trajectory**: <description, e.g. "5 → 5 → 4 → 4 → 4 across iter-100 to 104">
280	- **Helper accumulation**: <description, e.g. "13 helpers added across last 4 iters; 1 sorry closed">
281	- **Prover dispatch pattern**: <e.g. "1 of 3 ready files dispatched for 3 consecutive iters">
282	- **Recurring blockers**: <list or "none">
283	- **Avoidance patterns**: <list or "none" — name each: off-critical-path reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, persistent deferral language, possible rotation churn>
284	- **Prover status pattern**: <e.g. "PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL, PARTIAL">
285	- **Throughput**: ON_SCHEDULE | SLIPPING | OVER_BUDGET | ESTIMATE_FREE — <estimated K iters, elapsed K'>
286	- **Verdict**: CONVERGING | CHURNING | STUCK | UNCLEAR
287	- **Primary corrective** (if CHURNING/STUCK): <one of the actions above, with one paragraph of why>
288	- **Secondary correctives** (if applicable): <list>
289	
290	## PROGRESS.md dispatch sanity
291	
292	Independent of any route verdict.
293	
294	- **File count**: <N> (cap: <C>)
295	- **Ready but not dispatched**: <list of files with complete chapters and open sorries that are NOT in the proposal, or "none identified">
296	- **Over the cap**: yes | no
297	- **Under-dispatch finding**: yes | no — <if yes: gap size, how many consecutive iters>
298	- **Iter-over-iter trend**: <e.g. "1 → 1 → 1; consistently under-dispatching 3 ready files">
299	- **Verdict**: OK | OVER_CAP | UNDER_DISPATCH | BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS
300	  - OK: dispatch list is within cap, not under-dispatching ready files, and not growing while routes churn.
301	  - OVER_CAP: more files than the cap allows.
302	  - UNDER_DISPATCH: ≥3 ready files absent from the proposal, or consistently fewer than ready across ≥2 iters. Land in must-fix-this-iter.
303	  - BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS: file count growing iter over iter while route signals say CHURNING/STUCK.
304	
305	## Must-fix-this-iter <!-- omit entire section if no CHURNING/STUCK verdicts AND no OVER_BUDGET/OVER_CAP/UNDER_DISPATCH/BLOAT findings -->
306	
307	Every CHURNING and every STUCK verdict lands here automatically, every OVER_BUDGET throughput finding (with `Iters left > 0`), and every OVER_CAP / UNDER_DISPATCH / BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS dispatch verdict. Do not under-classify.
308	
309	- Route <name>: <verdict> — primary corrective: <action>. Why: <one line>.
310	- Route <name>: OVER_BUDGET — STRATEGY.md estimates <K> iters, elapsed <K'>. Revise the estimate or escalate.
311	- Route <name>: avoidance pattern — <specific pattern detected>. Primary corrective: <action>.
312	- Dispatch: UNDER_DISPATCH — <N> ready files absent from proposal for <M> consecutive iters. Fill all ready lanes this iter.
313	- Dispatch: OVER_CAP — planner listed <N> files (cap <C>). Re-prioritize; defer <N-C> files.
314	- Dispatch: BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS — file count <a> → <b> → <c> while routes <X>, <Y> remain CHURNING.
315	
316	## Informational <!-- omit if every route is CONVERGING with no commentary worth surfacing -->
317	
318	CONVERGING and UNCLEAR verdicts that warrant a comment.
319	
320	## Overall verdict
321	
322	One paragraph: how many routes are healthy, how many are stuck or churning or avoidance-stalled, what the planner's iter should look like to address the stuck ones. If avoidance patterns were detected, name them explicitly — "the planner has dispatched a single prover for 4 consecutive iters while 3 files were ready" must appear in the verdict so the plan agent cannot overlook it.
323	```
324	
325	## Return value
326	
327	Your final assistant message:
328	
329	- One line: `<slug>: <overall verdict> — <N> routes audited, <M> CHURNING/STUCK verdicts, <K> avoidance findings, dispatch=<OK|OVER_CAP|UNDER_DISPATCH|BLOAT_WITHOUT_PROGRESS>`
330	- The path to your full report.
331	
332	## Reminders
333	
334	- **You don't read strategy or blueprint.** Convergence is the question; soundness is for other subagents.
335	- **No bias toward CONVERGING.** The planner wants the route to be CONVERGING; you do not. Apply the verdict rules verbatim.
336	- **Under-dispatch is churn.** Sending one prover when three files are ready burns iterations just as surely as adding helpers that don't close sorries. Treat it as a planning failure, not a scheduling preference.
337	- **Avoidance is churn.** "Off-critical path" reclassification, consecutive plan-only iters, and persistent deferral language are all CHURNING signals. They indicate the planner is iterating around the hard problem instead of through it.
338	- **One primary corrective per route.** Don't list five and let the planner pick.
339	- **Recurring blockers are signal, not noise.** When the same blocker phrase appears across 3+ iters, the route is stuck regardless of helper counts.

1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.

5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.

13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.

17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.

21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.

26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.

Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. 9	/-!
10	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
11	
12	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
13	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
14	base change on the target.
15	
16	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
17	```
18	  X' --g'--> X
19	  |f'        |f
20	  v          v
21	  S' --g---> S
22	```
23	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
24	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
25	
26	The three main declarations are:
27	
28	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
29	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
30	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
31	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
32	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
33	  associativity).
34	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
35	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
36	
37	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
38	
39	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
40	Tag 02KH.
41	-/
42	
43	universe u
44	
45	open CategoryTheory Limits
46	
47	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
48	
49	open Scheme.Modules
50	
51	open scoped TensorProduct
52	
53	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
54	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
55	
56	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
57	
58	Given a commutative square
59	```
60	  X' --g'--> X
61	  |f'        |f
62	  v          v
63	  S' --g---> S
64	```
65	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
66	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
67	
68	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
69	```
70	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
71	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
72	```
73	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
74	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
75	commutativity of the square.
76	
77	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
78	base-change diagram. -/
79	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
80	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
81	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
82	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
83	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
84	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
85	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
86	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)

88	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — locality of isomorphisms for `Scheme.Modules`
89	
90	Mathlib provides the per-open criterion `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`
91	(`IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`) and the stalkwise criterion
92	`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` for `TopCat.Sheaf`-valued morphisms,
93	but it does not package the stalk-local criterion at the level of `Scheme.Modules`
94	morphisms. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they are the locality tools needed for
95	the affine reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (where one checks the
96	base-change map after restricting to / taking stalks over affine opens). -/
97	
98	/-- **Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism `φ : M ⟶ N`
99	of sheaves of modules over a scheme `X` is an isomorphism iff its underlying morphism of
100	abelian presheaves is a stalkwise isomorphism. Project-local: Mathlib only ships the
101	per-open criterion and the `TopCat.Sheaf`-level stalk criterion separately. -/
102	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
103	    (φ : M ⟶ N) :
104	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map
105	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by
106	  constructor
107	  · intro h x
108	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
109	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
110	  · intro h
111	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
112	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
113	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
114	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
115	    haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := h
116	    haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
117	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
118	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
119	    exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
120	
121	/-- **Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** If `B` is a basis of
122	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
123	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
124	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:
125	Mathlib provides the stalkwise pieces (`germ_exist_of_isBasis`,

729	`g^*(f_*(tilde M))` of the base-change map are canonically `R' ⊗_R M` as an `R'`-module — i.e.

731	`Θ_src` is the composite of the pushforward dictionary `pushforward_spec_tilde_iso` (reading
732	`f_*(tilde M)` as `restrictScalars φ M`) followed by the pullback dictionary
733	`pullback_spec_tilde_iso` (reading `g^*` of a tilde as extension of scalars along `ψ`), then the
734	tilde–Γ unit. The cospan maps `f = Spec φ`, `g = Spec ψ` are genuine `Spec`-maps, so the two
735	dictionaries apply directly with no pullback-leg identification needed. See blueprint
736	`lem:base_change_mate_domain_read`. -/
737	noncomputable def base_change_mate_domain_read {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}}
738	    (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) (M : ModuleCat.{u} A) :
739	    (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).obj
740	        ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map ψ)).obj
741	          ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Spec.map φ)).obj (tilde M))) ≅
742	      (ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M) :=
743	  (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).mapIso
744	      ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map ψ)).mapIso (pushforward_spec_tilde_iso φ M) ≪≫
745	        pullback_spec_tilde_iso ψ ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M)) ≪≫
746	    (tilde.toTildeΓNatIso.app
747	      ((ModuleCat.extendScalars ψ.hom).obj ((ModuleCat.restrictScalars φ.hom).obj M))).symm
748	
749	/-- **Pullback of modules along an isomorphism of schemes is an equivalence.** Project-local
750	helper: the inverse is `pullback (inv f)`, with unit/counit assembled from `pullbackComp` and
751	`pullbackId`. Used to recognise that pulling back along `pullbackSpecIso.hom` (an iso) and pushing
752	forward again returns the original module. -/
753	noncomputable def pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f] :
754	    Y.Modules ≌ X.Modules :=
755	  CategoryTheory.Equivalence.mk (Scheme.Modules.pullback f) (Scheme.Modules.pullback (inv f))
756	    ((Scheme.Modules.pullbackId Y).symm ≪≫
757	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (IsIso.inv_hom_id f).symm ≪≫
758	        (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp (inv f) f).symm)
759	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp f (inv f) ≪≫
760	      Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr (IsIso.hom_inv_id f) ≪≫ Scheme.Modules.pullbackId X)
761	
762	instance pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso {X Y : Scheme.{u}} (f : X ⟶ Y) [IsIso f] :
763	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback f).IsEquivalence :=
764	  (pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso f).isEquivalence_functor
765	
766	/-- **Codomain read of the section-level base-change map.** In the affine–affine model, with
767	`f' = pullback.snd` and `g' = pullback.fst` the legs of the pullback square, the global sections of
768	the codomain `f'_*(g')^*(tilde M)` are canonically `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` as an `R'`-module. The legs
769	are identified with the `Spec`-maps of the tensor inclusions by
770	`pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor` (L1), pulling the functors back to the affine `Spec (A ⊗_R R')`
771	chart, after which the two affine dictionaries apply. See blueprint
772	`lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read`. -/
773	noncomputable def base_change_mate_codomain_read {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}}
774	    (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) (M : ModuleCat.{u} A) :
775	    letI : Algebra (R : Type u) (A : Type u) := φ.hom.toAlgebra
776	    letI : Algebra (R : Type u) (R' : Type u) := ψ.hom.toAlgebra
777	    (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).obj
778	        ((Scheme.Modules.pushforward (Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))).obj
779	          ((Scheme.Modules.pullback (Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))).obj
780	            (tilde M))) ≅
781	      (ModuleCat.restrictScalars
782	          (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u))
783	            (B := (R' : Type u))).toRingHom).obj
784	        ((ModuleCat.extendScalars
785	          (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u))
786	            (B := (R' : Type u)))).obj M) := by
787	  letI : Algebra (R : Type u) (A : Type u) := φ.hom.toAlgebra
788	  letI : Algebra (R : Type u) (R' : Type u) := ψ.hom.toAlgebra
789	  set e := pullbackSpecIso (R := (R : Type u)) (S := (A : Type u)) (T := (R' : Type u)) with he
790	  set inclA := CommRingCat.ofHom (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeLeftRingHom
791	    (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u)) (B := (R' : Type u))) with hinclA
792	  set inclR' := CommRingCat.ofHom (Algebra.TensorProduct.includeRight
793	    (R := (R : Type u)) (A := (A : Type u)) (B := (R' : Type u))).toRingHom with hinclR'
794	  obtain ⟨hfst0, hsnd0⟩ := pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor ψ φ
795	  have hfst : Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ) = e.hom ≫ Spec.map inclA :=
796	    (Iso.inv_comp_eq e).mp hfst0
797	  have hsnd : Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ) = e.hom ≫ Spec.map inclR' :=
798	    (Iso.inv_comp_eq e).mp hsnd0
799	  set g' := Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ) with hg'
800	  set f' := Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ) with hf'
801	  set W₀ := (Scheme.Modules.pullback (Spec.map inclA)).obj (tilde M) with hW₀
802	  -- Read the pullback leg `g'` through `pullbackSpecIso` as the affine `Spec inclA` pullback.
803	  have iso_g : (Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj (tilde M)
804	        ≅ (Scheme.Modules.pullback e.hom).obj W₀ :=
805	    (Scheme.Modules.pullbackCongr hfst).app (tilde M) ≪≫
806	      (Scheme.Modules.pullbackComp e.hom (Spec.map inclA)).symm.app (tilde M)
807	  -- `e.hom` is an iso of schemes, so pulling back then pushing forward returns the module.

8	/-!
9	# Generic flatness of a coherent sheaf (A.2.a)
10	
11	This file packages the classical Grothendieck/Nitsure~\S4 **generic flatness**
12	theorem: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent `𝓞_X`-module on a
13	finite-type morphism `X ⟶ S` becomes flat after restricting to a non-empty open
14	subscheme of `S`. This is the inductive engine underlying the
15	flattening-stratification construction of Grothendieck's existence theorem for
16	the Quot scheme.
17	
18	Each blueprint-pinned declaration carries the *intended* substantive type
19	signature (matching the `\lean{...}` pin in
20	`blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`) with a `sorry`
21	body where the proof is not yet supplied. The substantive generic-flatness
22	proof is deep (Nitsure~\S4: prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
23	clearing denominators).
24	
25	The blueprint-pinned declaration is:
26	
27	- `AlgebraicGeometry.genericFlatness` (theorem) — **generic flatness**
28	  [Nitsure §4 Theorem]: over a noetherian integral base `S`, a coherent sheaf
29	  on a finite-type `X ⟶ S` is flat over a non-empty open `V ⊆ S`. Its
30	  algebraic form (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, no Lean pin) is the
31	  statement that a finite module over a finite-type algebra over a noetherian
32	  domain becomes free after inverting one non-zero element of the base.
33	
34	The file-internal `GenericFreeness` namespace lands the **finite-module /
35	finite-morphism special case** of the algebraic form axiom-clean, as a reusable
36	building block for the dévissage's leaves.
37	
38	## Note on type expressivity
39	
40	Following the project rule "Never weaken the type to dodge the proof",
41	`genericFlatness` requires the existence of a *non-empty* open `V` and
42	substantive flatness on every affine `U ⊆ V`; both quantifiers are necessary
43	(without them the statement collapses to `V = ∅`).
44	
45	## Mathlib status
46	
47	Mathlib (master `b80f227`) provides `Module.Flat`, `AlgebraicGeometry.Flat`
48	(morphism-level), `IsImmersion`, `IsLocallyNoetherian`, `IsIntegral`,
49	`LocallyOfFiniteType`, and the dévissage / Noether-normalisation infrastructure
50	the algebraic generic-flatness argument consumes. It does NOT yet provide the
51	polynomial-ring core of generic freeness, nor the geometric `genericFlatness`
52	statement packaging it over a noetherian integral base.
53	
54	## References
55	
56	Blueprint: `blueprint/src/chapters/Picard_FlatteningStratification.tex`.
57	Source: Nitsure, "Construction of Hilbert and Quot schemes", §4 (FGA Explained
58	Ch. 5, arXiv:math/0504020 pp. 5–18); Stacks Project tag 00HB (module flat).
59	-/

69	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — algebraic generic freeness (finite case)
70	
71	This section builds the **module-theoretic** generic-freeness statements that
72	underlie the geometric `genericFlatness` (blueprint
73	`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4 "Lemma on Generic Flatness").
74	
75	The full algebraic statement — `A` a noetherian domain, `B` a *finite-type*
76	`A`-algebra, `M` a finite `B`-module ⟹ `∃ f ≠ 0` with `M_f` free over `A_f` —
77	is a deep theorem (prime-filtration dévissage + Noether normalisation +
78	clearing denominators). Mathlib already supplies most of the dévissage
79	machinery (`IsNoetherianRing.induction_on_isQuotientEquivQuotientPrime`,
80	`ModuleCat.free_shortExact`, `exists_finite_inj_algHom_of_fg`), but it does
81	**not** yet contain the polynomial-ring core (generic freeness for a finite
82	module over `A[X₁,…,X_d]`). See the file `task_results` handoff for the
83	precise decomposition of the remaining gap.
84	
85	What we *can* land axiom-clean here is the **finite-module / finite-morphism
86	case**: when `M` is finite as an `A`-module (in particular when `B` is
87	module-finite over `A`), generic freeness follows directly from
88	`Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers` applied at the
89	generic point `Frac A`, where `M ⊗_A Frac A` is a finite vector space hence
90	free. This is a genuine special case of the algebraic generic-freeness
91	theorem (the case of a *finite* morphism `X → S`), and a reusable building
92	block for the dévissage's leaves. -/

96	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-module case.** For a noetherian integral domain
97	`A` and a finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` such that the
98	localisation `M_f` is free over `A_f = Localization.Away f`.
99	
100	This is the `d = 0` (finite-morphism) special case of the algebraic
101	generic-flatness theorem (`thm:generic_flatness_algebraic`, Nitsure~\S4):
102	inverting the generic point `Frac A`, the localised module is a finite vector
103	space hence free, and `Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers`
104	descends that freeness to a single basic open `D(f) ⊆ Spec A`. -/
105	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite
106	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
107	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
108	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
109	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
110	  haveI : Module.FinitePresentation A M := Module.finitePresentation_of_finite A M
111	  obtain ⟨r, hr, hfree, _⟩ :=
112	    Module.FinitePresentation.exists_free_localizedModule_powers (nonZeroDivisors A)
113	      (LocalizedModule.mkLinearMap (nonZeroDivisors A) M) (FractionRing A)
114	  exact ⟨r, nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hr, hfree⟩
115	
116	/-- **Generic flatness, finite-module case.** The flatness form of
117	`exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite`: for a noetherian domain `A` and a
118	finite `A`-module `M`, there is a non-zero `f` with `M_f` flat over `A_f`.
119	This is the affine-local content of `genericFlatness` for a finite morphism. -/
120	theorem exists_flat_localizationAway_of_finite
121	    (A : Type*) (M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
122	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module A M] [Module.Finite A M] :
123	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
124	      Module.Flat (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
125	  obtain ⟨f, hf, hfree⟩ := exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M
126	  haveI := hfree
127	  exact ⟨f, hf, Module.Flat.of_free⟩
128	
129	/-- **Generic freeness, finite-morphism case.** If `A` is a noetherian domain,
130	`B` a *module-finite* `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module (with the
131	compatible `A`-module structure), then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `M_f`
132	free over `A_f`. Reduces to `exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite` via
133	`Module.Finite.trans` (a finite module over a module-finite algebra is finite
134	over the base). This is generic flatness for a *finite* morphism `X → S`. -/
135	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_moduleFinite
136	    (A : Type*) (B : Type*) (M : Type*)
137	    [CommRing A] [IsDomain A] [IsNoetherianRing A]
138	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B] [Module.Finite A B]
139	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
140	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M] :
141	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
142	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
143	  haveI : Module.Finite A M := Module.Finite.trans B M
144	  exact exists_free_localizationAway_of_finite A M

146	/-! ### Dévissage chain for the finite-type residue (Nitsure §4)
147	
148	The following sub-lemmas decompose the surviving residue of
149	`genericFlatnessAlgebraic` — the case where `M` is finite over the finite-type
150	`A`-algebra `B` but not module-finite over `A` — into the classical Nitsure §4
151	induction. They are ordered so each step uses only earlier blocks (or Mathlib
152	anchors). Throughout, `A` is a noetherian domain with fraction field
153	`K = Frac A`, `B` a finite-type `A`-algebra, and `M` a finite `B`-module with
154	the compatible `A`-module structure via the scalar tower `A → B → M`. -/
155	
156	/-- **L1 — torsion base case `M_K = 0`** (`lem:gf_torsion_base`, Nitsure §4 base
157	case of the induction). If the generic localisation `M_K = K ⊗_A M` (encoded as
158	`LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M`) is trivial and `M` is a finite
159	`B`-module, then there is a non-zero `f ∈ A` with `f • M = 0`; consequently
160	`M_f = 0` is free over `A_f`.
161	
162	The hypothesis `Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M)` is the
163	formal encoding of `M_K = 0`: localising `M` at the non-zero divisors of the
164	domain `A` inverts exactly the non-zero elements, so `M_K = S^{-1}M` with
165	`S = A \ {0}`. Each generator of the finite `B`-module `M` is then killed by a
166	non-zero element of `A`; the product `f` of those annihilators (non-zero since
167	`A` is a domain) annihilates all of `M`, whence `M_f` is subsingleton and free. -/
168	theorem exists_free_localizationAway_of_torsion
169	    (A B M : Type*) [CommRing A] [IsDomain A]
170	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]
171	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module.Finite B M]
172	    [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M]
173	    (htors : Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (nonZeroDivisors A) M)) :
174	    ∃ f : A, f ≠ 0 ∧
175	      Module.Free (Localization.Away f) (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) := by
176	  -- The `A`/`B` scalar actions commute (A acts through `algebraMap A B`).
177	  have hcomm : ∀ (a : A) (b : B) (x : M), a • b • x = b • a • x := fun a b x => by
178	    rw [← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul B a (b • x), ← IsScalarTower.algebraMap_smul B a x,
179	      smul_smul, smul_smul, mul_comm]
180	  -- Torsion: every element of `M` is killed by a non-zero divisor of `A`.
181	  rw [LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff] at htors
182	  choose r hr using htors
183	  -- `r m ∈ A⁰` and `(r m) • m = 0`.
184	  -- A finite `B`-generating set of `M`.
185	  obtain ⟨S, hS⟩ := (Module.Finite.fg_top : (⊤ : Submodule B M).FG)
186	  -- The product of the chosen annihilators of the generators.
187	  set f : A := ∏ x ∈ S, r x with hf_def
188	  have hfmem : f ∈ nonZeroDivisors A := prod_mem fun x _ => (hr x).1
189	  have hf0 : f ≠ 0 := nonZeroDivisors.ne_zero hfmem
190	  -- `f` annihilates every generator, hence all of `span B S = ⊤ = M`.
191	  have hgen : ∀ x ∈ S, f • x = (0 : M) := by
192	    intro x hx
193	    classical
194	    rw [hf_def, ← Finset.prod_erase_mul S r hx, mul_smul, (hr x).2, smul_zero]
195	  have hfM : ∀ m : M, f • m = (0 : M) := by
196	    intro m
197	    have hmem : m ∈ Submodule.span B (↑S : Set M) := by rw [hS]; exact Submodule.mem_top
198	    induction hmem using Submodule.span_induction with
199	    | mem x hx => exact hgen x hx
200	    | zero => exact smul_zero f
201	    | add x y _ _ ihx ihy => rw [smul_add, ihx, ihy, add_zero]
202	    | smul b x _ ih => rw [hcomm, ih, smul_zero]
203	  -- `f • M = 0` ⟹ `M_f` subsingleton ⟹ free over `A_f`.
204	  refine ⟨f, hf0, ?_⟩
205	  haveI : Subsingleton (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) M) :=
206	    LocalizedModule.subsingleton_iff.mpr fun m => ⟨f, Submonoid.mem_powers f, hfM m⟩
207	  exact Module.Free.of_subsingleton _ _
208	
209	/-- **L3a — localisation of a short exact sequence is exact**
210	(`lem:gf_splice_shortExact_localized_exact`, Nitsure §4). Given a short exact
211	sequence `0 → M' → M → M'' → 0` of `B`-modules and `f ∈ A`, restricting scalars
212	along `A → B` and localising every term at the powers of `f` yields a short
213	exact sequence `0 → M'_f → M_f → M''_f → 0` of `A_f`-modules; the localised
214	`i_f` is injective and the localised `q_f` is surjective.
215	
216	Localisation of modules at a multiplicative set is exact, so it preserves the
217	injection `i`, the surjection `q`, and exactness at the middle term
218	(`LocalizedModule.map_injective` / `…map_surjective` / `…map_exact` applied to
219	the scalar-restricted maps). -/
220	theorem exact_localizedModule_powers_of_shortExact
221	    (A B M' M M'' : Type*) [CommRing A]
222	    [CommRing B] [Algebra A B]
223	    [AddCommGroup M'] [Module B M'] [Module A M'] [IsScalarTower A B M']
224	    [AddCommGroup M] [Module B M] [Module A M] [IsScalarTower A B M]
225	    [AddCommGroup M''] [Module B M''] [Module A M''] [IsScalarTower A B M'']
226	    (i : M' →ₗ[B] M) (q : M →ₗ[B] M'')
227	    (hi : Function.Injective i) (hq : Function.Surjective q)
228	    (hexact : Function.Exact i q)
229	    (f : A) :
230	    Function.Injective (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A)) ∧
231	    Function.Surjective (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (q.restrictScalars A)) ∧
232	    Function.Exact (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A))
233	      (LocalizedModule.map (Submonoid.powers f) (q.restrictScalars A)) := by
234	  refine ⟨LocalizedModule.map_injective _ (i.restrictScalars A) hi,
235	    LocalizedModule.map_surjective _ (q.restrictScalars A) hq, ?_⟩
236	  exact LocalizedModule.map_exact (Submonoid.powers f) (i.restrictScalars A)
237	    (q.restrictScalars A) hexact
238	
239	/-- **L3b — free transport across a finer localisation**
240	(`lem:gf_splice_shortExact_free_transport`, Nitsure §4). Let `N` be an
241	`A`-module and `f', f'' ∈ A` with `f = f' f''`. If `N_{f'}` is free over
242	`A_{f'}`, then `N_f` is free over `A_f`.
243	
244	Because `f = f' f''`, the localisation `A_f` is a localisation of `A_{f'}` at the
245	image of `f''` (`IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft` / `IsLocalization.Away.mul'`),
246	and `N_f` is the corresponding localisation of `N_{f'}`; a localisation of a free
247	module is free over the localised base ring (`Module.free_of_isLocalizedModule`). -/
248	theorem free_localizationAway_of_free_of_eq_mul
249	    (A N : Type*) [CommRing A] [AddCommGroup N] [Module A N]
250	    {f f' f'' : A} (hf : f = f' * f'')
251	    (hN' : Module.Free (Localization.Away f')
252	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f') N)) :
253	    Module.Free (Localization.Away f)
254	      (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f) N) := by
255	  subst hf
256	  haveI : Module.Free (Localization.Away f')
257	    (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers f') N) := hN'
258	  -- `A_f` also localises `f'' * f'` (= `f' * f''`).
259	  haveI hloc : IsLocalization.Away (f'' * f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) := by
260	    rw [mul_comm]; infer_instance
261	  -- The ring map `A_{f'} → A_{f'f''}` and the `A`-scalar-tower it sits in.
262	  letI algA' : Algebra (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) :=
263	    (IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft (R := A) (S := Localization.Away f')
264	      (P := Localization.Away (f' * f'')) f' f'').toAlgebra
265	  haveI htower : IsScalarTower A (Localization.Away f') (Localization.Away (f' * f'')) :=
266	    IsScalarTower.of_algebraMap_eq fun a =>
267	      (IsLocalization.Away.awayToAwayLeft_eq (S := Localization.Away f') f' f'' a).symm
268	  -- Restrict scalars along `A_{f'} → A_{f'f''}` to make `N_f` an `A_{f'}`-module.
269	  letI modA' : Module (Localization.Away f') (LocalizedModule (Submonoid.powers (f' * f'')) N) :=

33	/-! ## Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate (L4-a) -/
34	
35	/-- **Regrouping isomorphism, pure-tensor-algebra core.** For ring homomorphisms `ψ : R → R'`,
36	`φ : R → A` (giving `A` the `R`-algebra structure) and an `A`-module `M` (with `M` an `R`-module
37	via `φ` and `IsScalarTower R A M`), there is a bundled `R'`-linear isomorphism
38	\[ (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M, \]
39	where `R'` acts on the source through the `right`-algebra structure on `A ⊗[R] R'`
40	(`Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra`, i.e. the `includeRight` factor). On the generator it sends
41	`(1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m ↦ r' ⊗ m`; its inverse sends `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`. It carries **no** flatness
42	hypothesis.
43	
44	The construction is exactly the blueprint composite
45	`comm ≪≫ cancelBaseChange ≪≫ comm` (in the `A ⊗_R R'` orientation):
46	\[ (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M \xrightarrow{\texttt{comm}} M \otimes_A (A \otimes_R R')
47	   \xrightarrow{\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}} M \otimes_R R'
48	   \xrightarrow{\texttt{comm}} R' \otimes_R M, \]
49	assembled at the additive level from Mathlib's `TensorProduct.comm` (×2) and
50	`TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange` (instantiated at the tower `R → A → A` with
51	module `M` and base-change object `R'`), then re-bundled as an `R'`-linear equivalence by verifying
52	`R'`-`map_smul` on generators (`cancelBaseChange_tmul` plus the `rightAlgebra` action
53	`r' • (a ⊗ s) = a ⊗ (r' * s)`). This is the **mathematical heart** of the section-level mate
54	identification (`lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv`); the object-level packaging needed by the
55	chain is `base_change_mate_regroupEquiv` below. Axiom-clean (`propext`, `Quot.sound`).
56	
57	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma "Affine base change", proof, the
58	"boils down to the equality `(R' ⊗_R A) ⊗_A M = R' ⊗_R M`" step. -/
59	noncomputable def base_change_regroup_linearEquiv
60	    {R A R' : Type u} [CommRing R] [CommRing A] [CommRing R']
61	    [Algebra R A] [Algebra R R'] (M : Type u) [AddCommGroup M] [Module R M] [Module A M]
62	    [IsScalarTower R A M] :
63	    letI : Algebra R' (A ⊗[R] R') := Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra
64	    ((A ⊗[R] R') ⊗[A] M) ≃ₗ[R'] (R' ⊗[R] M) := by
65	  letI : Algebra R' (A ⊗[R] R') := Algebra.TensorProduct.rightAlgebra
66	  -- The underlying additive composite `comm ≫ cancelBaseChange ≫ comm`.
67	  let g : ((A ⊗[R] R') ⊗[A] M) ≃+ (R' ⊗[R] M) :=
68	    (TensorProduct.comm A (A ⊗[R] R') M).toAddEquiv.trans
69	      (((TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange R A A M R').toAddEquiv).trans
70	        (TensorProduct.comm R M R').toAddEquiv)
71	  refine { g with map_smul' := ?_ }
72	  intro r' x
73	  simp only [AddEquiv.toFun_eq_coe, RingHom.id_apply]
74	  induction x using TensorProduct.induction_on with
75	  | zero => simp
76	  | add a b ha hb => rw [smul_add, map_add, map_add, smul_add, ha, hb]
77	  | tmul t m =>
78	    induction t using TensorProduct.induction_on with
79	    | zero => simp
80	    | add a b ha hb => rw [TensorProduct.add_tmul, smul_add, map_add, map_add, smul_add, ha, hb]
81	    | tmul a s =>
82	      -- `r' • ((a ⊗ s) ⊗ m) = (a ⊗ (r' * s)) ⊗ m` via the `rightAlgebra` action on `A ⊗[R] R'`.
83	      have hsmul : r' • ((a ⊗ₜ[R] s) ⊗ₜ[A] m) = (a ⊗ₜ[R] (r' * s)) ⊗ₜ[A] m := by
84	        rw [TensorProduct.smul_tmul']
85	        congr 1
86	        rw [Algebra.smul_def, show (algebraMap R' (A ⊗[R] R')) r' = (1 : A) ⊗ₜ[R] r' from rfl,
87	          Algebra.TensorProduct.tmul_mul_tmul]
88	        simp
89	      rw [hsmul]
90	      change (TensorProduct.comm R M R') (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange
91	          R A A M R' ((TensorProduct.comm A (A ⊗[R] R') M) ((a ⊗ₜ[R] (r' * s)) ⊗ₜ[A] m)))
92	        = r' • g ((a ⊗ₜ[R] s) ⊗ₜ[A] m)
93	      show _ = r' • (TensorProduct.comm R M R') (TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange
94	          R A A M R' ((TensorProduct.comm A (A ⊗[R] R') M) ((a ⊗ₜ[R] s) ⊗ₜ[A] m)))
95	      simp only [TensorProduct.comm_tmul, TensorProduct.AlgebraTensorModule.cancelBaseChange_tmul,
96	        TensorProduct.smul_tmul']
97	      rw [smul_eq_mul]
98
